eli3: NLP (Natural Language Processing) in finance applies text analysis techniques to financial documents — earnings call transcripts, SEC filings, news articles, analyst reports, social media — to extract signals for trading or risk management. The core idea: the language used by executives, analysts, and media contains information about future company performance that is not fully captured in numerical data.

Key applications: predicting stock price movements from earnings call tone, detecting accounting fraud from unusual language in 10-K filings, measuring market sentiment from news headlines, and extracting information from unstructured documents (credit agreements, patents) that would take humans hours to read.

The field transformed dramatically with the advent of large language models (BERT, GPT). Domain-specific models like FinBERT, fine-tuned on financial text, significantly outperform general-purpose models and simple lexicon-based approaches on financial NLP tasks.

whenWhy.title: Earnings call analysis: tone, uncertainty, and deception
whenWhy.body: Earnings calls (quarterly) provide: (1) Prepared remarks: scripted, reviewed by legal; less informative; (2) Q&A session: spontaneous responses to analyst questions; more informative for detecting uncertainty, defensiveness, or evasion. Linguistic signals: Sentiment: Loughran-McDonald (LM) financial sentiment lexicon (2011) — 2,709 negative words, 354 positive words specifically calibrated to financial text (general sentiment lexicons like Harvard GI perform poorly on financial text; e.g., 'liability' is negative in general but neutral/positive in finance). Uncertainty: frequency of hedging language ('may,' 'might,' 'could,' 'possibly'); predicts future earnings volatility. Deception: linguistic patterns (increased complexity, fewer first-person pronouns, more distancing language) associated with fraudulent reporting (Larcker & Zakolyukina 2012). Processing: strip boilerplate text (safe harbour statements), split into prepared/Q&A sections, apply FinBERT for contextual sentiment.

whenWhy.title: 10-K filing analysis: MD&A and risk factors
whenWhy.body: 10-K filings (annual) contain: (1) Management Discussion & Analysis (MD&A): qualitative discussion of business performance; (2) Risk Factors: disclosure of material risks (mandated by SEC). Signals: (1) Year-over-year MD&A similarity (cosine similarity of tf-idf vectors): low similarity = management changed how they describe the business = structural shift; predicts negative future returns (Li 2010); (2) Risk factor count growth: more new risk factors = greater perceived uncertainty; (3) Tone of risk disclosure: more negative language in risk factors predicts future volatility; (4) Readability: complex, difficult-to-read filings (Fog Index) associated with lower quality/worse future performance (Li 2008) — managers may obscure bad news; (5) New risk factors: topics appearing in risk factors for the first time signal emerging problems (ESG risks, supply chain, cyber).

whenWhy.title: News and social media sentiment signals
whenWhy.body: News-based signals: (1) RavenPack: processes financial news in real-time; assigns relevance scores, sentiment scores, and event tags to each article for each mentioned company; (2) Bloomberg News Analytics (BNEF): similar platform integrated with Bloomberg Terminal; (3) Custom NLP: train a fine-tuned BERT classifier on labelled news (labelled by analysts as positive/negative/neutral for each named company). Short-horizon news signals: IC ≈ 0.05-0.15 for intraday and next-day returns; decays rapidly. Social media: (1) Twitter/X: investor attention, retail sentiment; high tweet volume = attention → future volatility; (2) Reddit WallStreetBets: retail momentum; correlated with options activity and short squeeze risk; (3) Google Trends: search volume for company name → attention proxy → predicts short-term volatility. Key challenge: distinguishing signal from noise in high-volume social media requires robust filtering for bots, spam, and coordinated manipulation.

deepDiveIntro: FinBERT: pre-trained BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) fine-tuned on financial text corpora (10-Ks, financial news, earnings call transcripts). Standard pipeline: tokenise text → FinBERT encoder → [CLS] token representation → fine-tuned classification head → sentiment (positive/negative/neutral) or other task. Performance on Financial PhraseBank: FinBERT accuracy ~87% vs. general BERT ~74% vs. LM lexicon ~64%. Implementation: HuggingFace library; model: 'ProsusAI/finbert'. Processing a full earnings call: ~5,000-20,000 tokens; requires chunking (BERT max 512 tokens); approaches: (1) sliding window + pooling; (2) hierarchical model (sentence-level → document-level); (3) use only Q&A section (most informative).

deepDive.heading: Loughran-McDonald Lexicon vs. FinBERT: When to Use Which
deepDive.body: Loughran-McDonald (LM) lexicon: count-based approach; each word has a pre-assigned sentiment label; sentiment score = (positive words - negative words) / total words. Advantages: fast, transparent, no model to train, works on small datasets. Disadvantages: ignores context ('not great' counted as positive + negative words); misses domain-specific nuances; doesn't capture negation, sarcasm, or hedging. FinBERT: contextual transformer model; processes full sentence meaning; handles negation ('revenues did not exceed expectations'), complex structures, and domain jargon naturally. Advantages: much higher accuracy (10-15% improvement on most tasks). Disadvantages: slow (GPU needed for large-scale processing), black-box, requires fine-tuning data for specific tasks. When to use LM: high-volume real-time processing where speed matters; when you need strict reproducibility and auditability; when text is short and clean (news headlines). When to use FinBERT: earnings call analysis, 10-K analysis, longer-horizon research where accuracy matters more than speed. [1]

deepDive.heading: Large Language Models for Financial Document Analysis
deepDive.body: GPT-4, Claude, and Llama models enable new financial NLP applications beyond classification: (1) Document summarisation: 100-page 10-K → 2-page summary of key financial developments and risks; (2) Comparison: 'How did Company A's risk factors change vs. last year? What new risks appeared?'; (3) Covenant extraction: from credit agreements (unstructured legal documents), extract key covenants and thresholds; (4) Earnings call Q&A: identify which analyst asked the most probing questions; flag CEO responses that are unusually evasive; (5) Financial statement parsing: extract specific line items from PDFs/images using OCR + LLM; (6) Earnings prediction: 'Given this 10-K text, predict whether next quarter EPS will beat consensus.' LLM limitations for finance: hallucination risk (LLMs can fabricate financial figures); knowledge cutoff (no real-time data); cost (GPT-4 API at scale is expensive); and difficulty of systematic backtesting LLM outputs. Validated approach: use LLMs for feature extraction (entity recognition, relationship extraction), then feed structured features into traditional quantitative models. [2]

deepDive.heading: SEC Full-Text Search and Fraud Detection
deepDive.body: SEC EDGAR full-text search: all public company filings are available via EDGAR (SEC's database). SEC full-text search API allows querying for specific terms across millions of filings. Applications: (1) Fraud detection: Beneish M-score is a numerical model; NLP adds a text dimension — companies committing fraud tend to: use more abstract language (fewer concrete nouns), avoid first-person pronouns, increase use of complex sentences, and use unusual jargon to obscure operations; (2) ESG risk identification: scan 10-K risk factors for ESG-related terms (climate change, diversity, regulatory) to build ESG exposure scores before formal ESG ratings; (3) Litigation risk: scan 8-K filings and legal proceedings section for new litigation; companies that first disclose litigation in filings experience subsequent stock underperformance; (4) Patent analysis: measure R&D productivity by the number and quality (citation count) of patents filed; high-quality patent portfolio → future competitive advantage → positive signal. Tools: EDGAR API (free), WRDS SEC Analytics Suite (institutional), LexisNexis (legal documents). [1]

takeaways: LM lexicon: fast, transparent, 2,709 negative / 354 positive financial words; use for high-volume real-time. [1]
FinBERT: BERT fine-tuned on financial text; 87% accuracy on FPB vs. 74% general BERT; use for research. [1]
10-K signals: YoY MD&A similarity (cosine), readability (Fog Index), risk factor count — all predict future performance. [1]
Earnings call Q&A: uncertainty language, deception markers, management tone — stronger signals than prepared remarks. [1]
LLMs enable qualitative document analysis at scale — but use for feature extraction, not direct predictions. [2]

edgeCases.q: How do you handle multilingual financial documents in a global portfolio?
edgeCases.a: For a global equity portfolio, companies report in their local language (Japanese, German, Chinese, etc.) alongside or instead of English. Approaches: (1) Machine translation: translate to English, then apply English NLP models. Quality is high for major languages (Japanese, German, French, Chinese); use DeepL or Google Translate API. Risk: nuanced financial language may not translate perfectly; legal terms have country-specific meanings; (2) Multilingual BERT/XLM-RoBERTa: pre-trained on 100+ languages; fine-tune on multilingual financial text for classification tasks; avoids translation step; (3) Language-specific lexicons: Loughran-McDonald-inspired lexicons developed for German (Banken & Hemmeter 2016), Japanese, and Chinese financial text; simpler but lower accuracy; (4) Local data providers: for Asian markets, Bloomberg provides Chinese/Japanese NLP signals; local providers (Wind for China, Nikkei for Japan) offer pre-processed sentiment. Coverage gap: emerging market companies have less English-language text coverage; NLP signals are stronger for developed markets.

edgeCases.q: Can NLP detect accounting manipulation before it becomes public?
edgeCases.a: Several studies show NLP features have incremental predictive power for detecting accounting irregularities before formal discovery: (1) Larcker & Zakolyukina (2012): CEO/CFO language in earnings calls predicts future restatements. Deception markers: fewer confident first-person pronouns ('we achieved' → 'the company achieved'), more hedging, fewer references to shareholder value creation; (2) Bao & Datta (2014): 10-K text complexity (longer sentences, more passive voice, more unique words) predicts future SEC enforcement actions; (3) Perols & Lougee (2011): MD&A tone + numerical red flags (Beneish M-score) combined outperform either alone for fraud prediction. Practical limits: (1) Base rate is very low — financial fraud in large-cap companies is rare (~1% per year); even a model with 80% accuracy produces many false positives; (2) Management teams learn to game language patterns over time; (3) Legal/ethical considerations: acting on fraud predictions before public announcement is legal only if based on public data.

sources.label: Loughran, T. & McDonald, B. (2011) — When Is a Liability Not a Liability?, JF
Yang, Y. et al. (2020) — FinBERT: A Pre-Trained Financial Language Representation Model, IJCAI
